speaker: POET
text: Good day, sir.

speaker: PAINTER
text: I am glad you’re well.

speaker: POET
text: I have not seen you long. How goes the world?

speaker: PAINTER
text: It wears, sir, as it grows.

speaker: POET
text: Ay, that’s well known.

speaker: POET
text: But what particular rarity, what strange,

speaker: POET
text: Which manifold record not matches? See,

speaker: POET
text: Magic of bounty, all these spirits thy power

speaker: POET
text: Hath conjured to attend. I know the merchant.

speaker: PAINTER
text: I know them both. Th’ other’s a jeweler.

speaker: MERCHANT
text: O, ’tis a worthy lord!

speaker: JEWELER
text: Nay, that’s most fixed.

speaker: MERCHANT
text: A most incomparable man, breathed, as it were,

speaker: MERCHANT
text: To an untirable and continuate goodness.

speaker: MERCHANT
text: He passes.

speaker: JEWELER
text: I have a jewel here—

speaker: MERCHANT
text: O, pray, let’s see ’t. For the Lord Timon, sir?

speaker: JEWELER
text: If he will touch the estimate. But for that—

speaker: POET
text: When we for recompense have praised the vile,

speaker: POET
text: It stains the glory in that happy verse

speaker: POET
text: Which aptly sings the good.

speaker: MERCHANT
text: ’Tis a good form.

speaker: JEWELER
text: And rich. Here is a water, look ye.

speaker: PAINTER
text: You are rapt, sir, in some work, some dedication

speaker: PAINTER
text: To the great lord.

speaker: POET
text: A thing slipped idly from me.

speaker: POET
text: Our poesy is as a gum which oozes

speaker: POET
text: From whence ’tis nourished. The fire i’ th’ flint

speaker: POET
text: Shows not till it be struck; our gentle flame

speaker: POET
text: Provokes itself and, like the current, flies

speaker: POET
text: Each bound it chases. What have you there?

speaker: PAINTER
text: A picture, sir. When comes your book forth?

speaker: POET
text: Upon the heels of my presentment, sir.

speaker: POET
text: Let’s see your piece.

speaker: PAINTER
text: ’Tis a good piece.

speaker: POET
text: So ’tis. This comes off well and excellent.

speaker: PAINTER
text: Indifferent.

speaker: POET
text: Admirable! How this grace

speaker: POET
text: Speaks his own standing! What a mental power

speaker: POET
text: This eye shoots forth! How big imagination

speaker: POET
text: Moves in this lip! To th’ dumbness of the gesture

speaker: POET
text: One might interpret.

speaker: PAINTER
text: It is a pretty mocking of the life.

speaker: PAINTER
text: Here is a touch. Is ’t good?

speaker: POET
text: I will say of it,

speaker: POET
text: It tutors nature. Artificial strife

speaker: POET
text: Lives in these touches livelier than life.

speaker: PAINTER
text: How this lord is followed.

speaker: POET
text: The senators of Athens, happy men.

speaker: PAINTER
text: Look, more.

speaker: POET
text: You see this confluence, this great flood of visitors.

speaker: POET
text:
(
                Indicating
                 
                his
                 
                poem
                .
                )
                 
              I have in this rough work shaped out a man

speaker: POET
text: Whom this beneath world doth embrace and hug

speaker: POET
text: With amplest entertainment. My free drift

speaker: POET
text: Halts not particularly but moves itself

speaker: POET
text: In a wide sea of wax. No leveled malice

speaker: POET
text: Infects one comma in the course I hold,

speaker: POET
text: But flies an eagle flight, bold and forth on,

speaker: POET
text: Leaving no tract behind.

speaker: PAINTER
text: How shall I understand you?

speaker: POET
text: I will unbolt to you.

speaker: POET
text: You see how all conditions, how all minds,

speaker: POET
text: As well of glib and slipp’ry creatures as

speaker: POET
text: Of grave and austere quality, tender down

speaker: POET
text: Their services to Lord Timon. His large fortune,

speaker: POET
text: Upon his good and gracious nature hanging,

speaker: POET
text: Subdues and properties to his love and tendance

speaker: POET
text: All sorts of hearts—yea, from the glass-faced flatterer

speaker: POET
text: To Apemantus, that few things loves better

speaker: POET
text: Than to abhor himself; even he drops down

speaker: POET
text: The knee before him and returns in peace

speaker: POET
text: Most rich in Timon’s nod.

speaker: PAINTER
text: I saw them speak together.

speaker: POET
text: Sir, I have upon a high and pleasant hill

speaker: POET
text: Feigned Fortune to be throned. The base o’ th’ mount

speaker: POET
text: Is ranked with all deserts, all kind of natures

speaker: POET
text: That labor on the bosom of this sphere

speaker: POET
text: To propagate their states. Amongst them all

speaker: POET
text: Whose eyes are on this sovereign lady fixed,

speaker: POET
text: One do I personate of Lord Timon’s frame,

speaker: POET
text: Whom Fortune with her ivory hand wafts to her,

speaker: POET
text: Whose present grace to present slaves and servants

speaker: POET
text: Translates his rivals.

speaker: PAINTER
text: ’Tis conceived to scope.

speaker: PAINTER
text: This throne, this Fortune, and this hill, methinks,

speaker: PAINTER
text: With one man beckoned from the rest below,

speaker: PAINTER
text: Bowing his head against the steepy mount

speaker: PAINTER
text: To climb his happiness, would be well expressed

speaker: PAINTER
text: In our condition.

speaker: POET
text: Nay, sir, but hear me on.

speaker: POET
text: All those which were his fellows but of late,

speaker: POET
text: Some better than his value, on the moment

speaker: POET
text: Follow his strides, his lobbies fill with tendance,

speaker: POET
text: Rain sacrificial whisperings in his ear,

speaker: POET
text: Make sacred even his stirrup, and through him

speaker: POET
text: Drink the free air.

speaker: PAINTER
text: Ay, marry, what of these?

speaker: POET
text: When Fortune in her shift and change of mood

speaker: POET
text: Spurns down her late beloved, all his dependants,

speaker: POET
text: Which labored after him to the mountain’s top

speaker: POET
text: Even on their knees and hands, let him slip down,

speaker: POET
text: Not one accompanying his declining foot.

speaker: PAINTER
text: ’Tis common.

speaker: PAINTER
text: A thousand moral paintings I can show

speaker: PAINTER
text: That shall demonstrate these quick blows of Fortune’s

speaker: PAINTER
text: More pregnantly than words. Yet you do well

speaker: PAINTER
text: To show Lord Timon that mean eyes have seen

speaker: PAINTER
text: The foot above the head.

speaker: TIMON
text: Imprisoned is he, say you?

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Ay, my good lord. Five talents is his debt,

speaker: MESSENGER
text: His means most short, his creditors most strait.

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Your honorable letter he desires

speaker: MESSENGER
text: To those have shut him up, which failing

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Periods his comfort.

speaker: TIMON
text: Noble Ventidius. Well,

speaker: TIMON
text: I am not of that feather to shake off

speaker: TIMON
text: My friend when he must need me. I do know him

speaker: TIMON
text: A gentleman that well deserves a help,

speaker: TIMON
text: Which he shall have. I’ll pay the debt and free him.

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Your Lordship ever binds him.

speaker: TIMON
text: Commend me to him. I will send his ransom;

speaker: TIMON
text: And, being enfranchised, bid him come to me.

speaker: TIMON
text: ’Tis not enough to help the feeble up,

speaker: TIMON
text: But to support him after. Fare you well.

speaker: MESSENGER
text: All happiness to your Honor.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Lord Timon, hear me speak.

speaker: TIMON
text: Freely, good father.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Thou hast a servant named Lucilius.

speaker: TIMON
text: I have so. What of him?

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Most noble Timon, call the man before thee.

speaker: TIMON
text: Attends he here or no?—Lucilius!

speaker: LUCILIUS
text: Here, at your Lordship’s service.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: This fellow here, Lord Timon, this thy creature,

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: By night frequents my house. I am a man

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: That from my first have been inclined to thrift,

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: And my estate deserves an heir more raised

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Than one which holds a trencher.

speaker: TIMON
text: Well. What further?

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: One only daughter have I, no kin else

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: On whom I may confer what I have got.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: The maid is fair, o’ th’ youngest for a bride,

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: And I have bred her at my dearest cost

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: In qualities of the best. This man of thine

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Attempts her love. I prithee, noble lord,

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Join with me to forbid him her resort.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Myself have spoke in vain.

speaker: TIMON
text: The man is honest.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Therefore he will be, Timon.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: His honesty rewards him in itself;

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: It must not bear my daughter.

speaker: TIMON
text: Does she love him?

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: She is young and apt.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Our own precedent passions do instruct us

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: What levity’s in youth.

speaker: TIMON
text: Love you the maid?

speaker: LUCILIUS
text: Ay, my good lord, and she accepts of it.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: If in her marriage my consent be missing—

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: I call the gods to witness—I will choose

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Mine heir from forth the beggars of the world

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: And dispossess her all.

speaker: TIMON
text: How shall she be endowed

speaker: TIMON
text: If she be mated with an equal husband?

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Three talents on the present; in future, all.

speaker: TIMON
text: This gentleman of mine hath served me long.

speaker: TIMON
text: To build his fortune, I will strain a little,

speaker: TIMON
text: For ’tis a bond in men. Give him thy daughter.

speaker: TIMON
text: What you bestow, in him I’ll counterpoise,

speaker: TIMON
text: And make him weigh with her.

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Most noble lord,

speaker:
OLD
               
              MAN
text: Pawn me to this your honor, she is his.

speaker: TIMON
text: My hand to thee; mine honor on my promise.

speaker: LUCILIUS
text: Humbly I thank your Lordship. Never may

speaker: LUCILIUS
text: That state or fortune fall into my keeping

speaker: LUCILIUS
text: Which is not owed to you.

speaker: POET
text: Vouchsafe my labor, and long live your Lordship.

speaker: TIMON
text: I thank you. You shall hear from me anon.

speaker: TIMON
text: Go not away.—What have you there, my friend?

speaker: PAINTER
text: A piece of painting which I do beseech

speaker: PAINTER
text: Your Lordship to accept.

speaker: TIMON
text: Painting is welcome.

speaker: TIMON
text: The painting is almost the natural man,

speaker: TIMON
text: For, since dishonor traffics with man’s nature,

speaker: TIMON
text: He is but outside; these penciled figures are

speaker: TIMON
text: Even such as they give out. I like your work,

speaker: TIMON
text: And you shall find I like it. Wait attendance

speaker: TIMON
text: Till you hear further from me.

speaker: PAINTER
text: The gods preserve you.

speaker: TIMON
text: Well fare you, gentleman. Give me your hand.

speaker: TIMON
text: We must needs dine together.—Sir, your jewel

speaker: TIMON
text: Hath suffered under praise.

speaker: JEWELER
text: What, my lord? Dispraise?

speaker: TIMON
text: A mere satiety of commendations.

speaker: TIMON
text: If I should pay you for ’t as ’tis extolled,

speaker: TIMON
text: It would unclew me quite.

speaker: JEWELER
text: My lord, ’tis rated

speaker: JEWELER
text: As those which sell would give. But you well know

speaker: JEWELER
text: Things of like value, differing in the owners,

speaker: JEWELER
text: Are prizèd by their masters. Believe ’t, dear lord,

speaker: JEWELER
text: You mend the jewel by the wearing it.

speaker: TIMON
text: Well mocked.

speaker: MERCHANT
text: No, my good lord. He speaks the common tongue,

speaker: MERCHANT
text: Which all men speak with him.

speaker: TIMON
text: Look who comes here. Will you be chid?

speaker: JEWELER
text: We’ll bear, with your Lordship.

speaker: MERCHANT
text: He’ll spare none.

speaker: TIMON
text: Good morrow to thee, gentle Apemantus.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Till I be gentle, stay thou for thy good morrow—

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: When thou art Timon’s dog, and these knaves honest.

speaker: TIMON
text: Why dost thou call them knaves? Thou know’st them not.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Are they not Athenians?

speaker: TIMON
text: Yes.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Then I repent not.

speaker: JEWELER
text: You know me, Apemantus?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Thou know’st I do. I called thee by thy

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: name.

speaker: TIMON
text: Thou art proud, Apemantus.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Of nothing so much as that I am not like

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Timon.

speaker: TIMON
text: Whither art going?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: To knock out an honest Athenian’s brains.

speaker: TIMON
text: That’s a deed thou ’lt die for.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Right, if doing nothing be death by th’ law.

speaker: TIMON
text: How lik’st thou this picture, Apemantus?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: The best, for the innocence.

speaker: TIMON
text: Wrought he not well that painted it?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: He wrought better that made the painter,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: and yet he’s but a filthy piece of work.

speaker: PAINTER
text: You’re a dog.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Thy mother’s of my generation. What’s

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: she, if I be a dog?

speaker: TIMON
text: Wilt dine with me, Apemantus?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: No. I eat not lords.

speaker: TIMON
text: An thou shouldst, thou ’dst anger ladies.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: O, they eat lords. So they come by great

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: bellies.

speaker: TIMON
text: That’s a lascivious apprehension.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: So thou apprehend’st it. Take it for thy

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: labor.

speaker: TIMON
text: How dost thou like this jewel, Apemantus?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Not so well as plain-dealing, which will

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: not cost a man a doit.

speaker: TIMON
text: What dost thou think ’tis worth?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Not worth my thinking.—How now, poet?

speaker: POET
text: How now, philosopher?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Thou liest.

speaker: POET
text: Art not one?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Yes.

speaker: POET
text: Then I lie not.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Art not a poet?

speaker: POET
text: Yes.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Then thou liest. Look in thy last work,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: where thou hast feigned him a worthy fellow.

speaker: POET
text: That’s not feigned. He is so.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Yes, he is worthy of thee, and to pay thee

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: for thy labor. He that loves to be flattered is worthy

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: o’ th’ flatterer. Heavens, that I were a lord!

speaker: TIMON
text: What wouldst do then, Apemantus?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: E’en as Apemantus does now—hate a lord

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: with my heart.

speaker: TIMON
text: What? Thyself?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Ay.

speaker: TIMON
text: Wherefore?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: That I had no angry wit to be a lord.—Art

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: not thou a merchant?

speaker: MERCHANT
text: Ay, Apemantus.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Traffic confound thee, if the gods will not.

speaker: MERCHANT
text: If traffic do it, the gods do it.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Traffic’s thy god, and thy god confound

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: thee!

speaker: TIMON
text: What trumpet’s that?

speaker: MESSENGER
text: ’Tis Alcibiades and some twenty horse,

speaker: MESSENGER
text: All of companionship.

speaker: TIMON
text: Pray, entertain them. Give them guide to us.

speaker: TIMON
text: You must needs dine with me. Go not you hence

speaker: TIMON
text: Till I have thanked you.—When dinner’s done

speaker: TIMON
text: Show me this piece.—I am joyful of your sights.

speaker: TIMON
text: Most welcome, sir.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: So, so, there!

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Aches contract and starve your supple joints!

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: That there should be small love amongst these sweet knaves,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: And all this courtesy! The strain of man’s bred out

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Into baboon and monkey.

speaker: ALCIBIADES
text: Sir, you have saved my longing, and I feed

speaker: ALCIBIADES
text: Most hungerly on your sight.

speaker: TIMON
text: Right welcome, sir.

speaker: TIMON
text: Ere we depart, we’ll share a bounteous time

speaker: TIMON
text: In different pleasures. Pray you, let us in.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: What time o’ day is ’t, Apemantus?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Time to be honest.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: That time serves still.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: The most accursèd thou, that still omit’st it.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Thou art going to Lord Timon’s feast?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Ay, to see meat fill knaves, and wine heat fools.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Fare thee well, fare thee well.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Thou art a fool to bid me farewell twice.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Why, Apemantus?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Shouldst have kept one to thyself, for I mean to give thee none.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Hang thyself.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: No, I will do nothing at thy bidding.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Make thy requests to thy friend.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Away, unpeaceable dog, or I’ll spurn thee hence.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: I will fly, like a dog, the heels o’ th’ ass.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: He’s opposite to humanity. Come, shall we in

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: And taste Lord Timon’s bounty? He outgoes

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: The very heart of kindness.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: He pours it out. Plutus, the god of gold,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Is but his steward. No meed but he repays

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Sevenfold above itself. No gift to him

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: But breeds the giver a return exceeding

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: All use of quittance.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: The noblest mind he carries

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: That ever governed man.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Long may he live in fortunes. Shall we in?

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: I’ll keep you company.
